Research ArticleOpen Access

Climate Adaptation in South Asian Livestock Production Systems: Emerging Trends and Contextual Insights for Policy Imperatives

[version 1]

Sanjit Maiti

, 

Amitava Panja

, 

Aniket Deo

, 

Kaushik Bora

, 

Sanchita Garai

, 

Paresh Shirsath

, 

CA Rama Rao

, 

Pramod K Aggarwal

This article is included in Gates Foundation gateway

Abstract

The South Asia region is facing the impact of rapidly escalating climate change with events such as rising temperatures, erratic precipitation and associated disasters. Climate change poses a significant challenge to livestock-based livelihoods in South Asia, affecting productivity, reproduction rates, and food security. Despite farmers adopting various adaptation strategies, a gap exists in comprehensively identifying and exploring the rationale behind these strategies in the literature dealing with climate adaptation strategies. The objective of this study is to systematically review existing literature on climate change adaptation strategies that have been adopted by livestock rearers in the South Asia region. It synthesized and analysed South Asian livestock rearers’ climate adaptation strategies using a PRISMA-based Systematic Literature Review (SLR). A total of 41 peer-reviewed research articles were selected from Scopus, Web of Science, Lens, and Google Scholar. Adaptation strategies were categorized under eight thematic domains. Bibliometric analysis indicated a significant rise in publication patterns, particularly post-2010, with India leading in contributions. Keyword network analysis underscored the importance of ‘adaptation’ and ‘climate change’, illustrating its critical influence on research related to climate resilience, livestock management, and farmers’ perspectives. Research since 2018 highlighted the increasing complexity of adaptation strategies, incorporating elements such as climate-smart agriculture and risk management. Thermal stress management and fodder management strategies were most prevalent. Strategies were both farmer-led and advocated by them, with most being implemented spontaneously by farmers. This study identified the climate adaptation strategies adopted by livestock rearers in South Asian countries. Secondly, it also explores the rationale behind the use, and links it to further policy advocation.

Corresponding Author: Sanjit Maiti
Competing Interests: No competing interests were disclosed.
Grant Information: This work is an output of the project "Atlas of Climate Adaptation in South Asia (ACASA)" funded in part by the Gates Foundation (Grant No. INV-042997).
The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

First Version Published: 11 Nov 2025, 2:373 (https://doi.org/10.12688/verixiv.2338.1)
Latest Version Published: 11 Nov 2025, 2:373 (https://doi.org/10.12688/verixiv.2338.1)

Key policy insights

  • • Climate-smart livestock adaptation should be mainstreamed withing national climate policies through region-specific pathways integrating farmers’ needs, indigenous knowledge systems, and innovations thereby facilitating knowledge co-production.

  • • Climate based insurance, concessional credit, and subsidies in context of livestock sector must be expanded to enhance smallholders’ adaptive capacity and reduce livelihood losses due to climate-induced shocks.

  • • Investment in climate smart infrastructure, decentralized veterinary services, and early warning systems is essential to strengthen resilience and accelerate diffusion of climate adaptation strategies.

  • • Livestock adaptation should be integrated into disaster risk reduction frameworks, supported by gender-responsive extension and community capacity building to strengthen resilience and transfer of technology.

1. Introduction

The earth’s climate system is changing in ways never seen before due to human-caused global warming. Climate change seriously affects agricultural sustainability and poverty alleviation in rural and vulnerable regions, resulting in increased poverty, decreased human and physical capital development, inadequate infrastructural development, and unequal land distribution (Ikehi et al., 2014). The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) reports that climate change has significantly harmed the agriculture sector, causing food insecurity and a drop in living standards for millions (IPCC, 2014). Research indicates that the frequency and severity of weather occurrences would increase simultaneously as a result of increases in surface temperature and sea levels. Meanwhile, the global population is expected to rise to 9.8 billion by 2050 (United Nations, 2017) leading to a critical food security problem, especially in developing nations (Wheeler & von Braun, 2013; Zewdie, 2012).

India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, Maldives, and Afghanistan hold nearly one-fifth of the world’s population and is the most densely inhabited region. Increasing climate change is also a threat to this region (S. Singh et al., 2021b). The region has been witnessing a warming trend of about 0.75°C in annual mean temperatures in the past century (M. Ahmed & Suphachalasai, 2014). The annual mean temperature is anticipated to rise by 0.9°C, 0.9°C, and 1.2°C for SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, and SSP5-8.5 respectively, between 2030 and 2049, indicating severe global warming in South Asia (Almazroui et al., 2020). Heat stress in this region is projected to increase by 12% in 2030 and 21% in 2050 (Tesfaye et al., 2017). Rainfall anomaly has been observed across South Asia and is expected to increase by 10-20% in annual mean precipitation across all three socio-shared economic routes (Almazroui et al., 2020). Disease outbreaks, floods, and landslides in hilly regions are all more likely to occur in correlation with an increase in precipitation intensity (Naveendrakumar et al., 2019). Additionally, variations in the long-term precipitation patterns of South Asian countries may contribute to the intensification of tropical cyclone activity in the north Indian Ocean (Takahashi, 2011). According to Kumar (2020), one of the precursors of climate change in South Asia is attributed to global sea level rise, and with a 5-metre rise in sea level, it is very likely that there will be over 125 million climate migrants in this region alone (Byravan & Rajan, 2008).

Agriculture and allied sectors form the backbone of South Asia’s rural economy, supporting millions of livelihoods and ensuring food security (B. B. Singh et al., 2021a). Therefore, climate change will have a devastating impact on South Asian agriculture. In addition to the direct impacts of climate change on agriculture, the livestock sector will also be significantly impacted (Shinde & Sejian, 2013; Sirohi & Michaelowa, 2007). Climate-induced heat stress, humidity fluctuations, and solar radiation negatively affect livestock by altering growth rates, milk production, reproductive performance, metabolic functions, and disease susceptibility (Pankaj et al., 2013). Feed and water shortages, exacerbated by rising temperatures and erratic precipitation, further threaten livestock productivity (Rojas-Downing et al., 2017; Sejian et al., 2015). The potential impacts of climate change on the livestock sector were explained methodically by Rojas-Downing et al. (2017) ( Figure 1).

Figure 1. Impacts of climate change on the livestock sector.

Source: Rojas-Downing et al. 2017.

Since the majority of South Asian farmers are small and marginal, and they practice mixed farming involving crop cultivation and livestock rearing (Devendra & Thomas, 2002), the effects of climate change on livestock farming in South Asia threaten overall food security. In South Asia, 41.3% of the population experienced moderate or severe food insecurity between 2020 and 2022, highlighting the urgent need for adaptive strategies in livestock farming (FAO, n.d.).

Given the diverse agro-climatic zones across South Asia, it is crucial to understand the adaptive measures employed by livestock rearers in response to climate change. While previous studies have systematically reviewed climate adaptation strategies in crop farming (Aryal et al., 2020) or conducted country-specific analyses (Aggarwal, 2008; Chowdhury et al., 2022; Kundu et al., 2020; Rijal et al., 2022; Saddique et al., 2022) or region wise scrutiny (Panja et al., 2024), no study has systematically examined adaptation strategies in the livestock sector at a regional scale. Addressing this research gap, this study explores how livestock farmers in South Asia adapt to climate change and build resilience in their production systems. This brings us to a serious question: How is South Asia’s livestock sector adapting to climate change challenges? So this study revolves around a single research question, i.e., What are the adaptation strategies followed by livestock farmers in South Asia to cope with climate change? Through a comprehensive and systematic review, this study aims to identify and categorize adaptation strategies based on different domains of livestock management, comprehensively explores the effectiveness of various adaptation practices in reducing climate-induced risks, and highlights policy recommendations that enhance resilience in the livestock sector. By synthesizing evidence-based insights, this research provides a foundation for policy development, sustainable livestock management, and targeted climate interventions in South Asia. The analysis will also help in framing a realistic policy for a climate-resilient South Asian livestock sector.

2. Methodology

This systematic review was conducted following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) framework (Moher et al., 2009; Pickering et al., 2015; Pickering & Byrne, 2014). The primary objective of this approach was to synthesise, assess, and monitor the relevant scientific literature on a specific research topic. To mitigate potential biases, the review process involved selecting and categorising studies based on clearly defined eligibility criteria. The rigour and transparency of a systematic review are enhanced when inclusion and exclusion criteria are explicitly formulated and consistently applied (Berrang-Ford et al., 2015; McDowell et al., 2014). Once the research topic was established, the inclusion and exclusion criteria were applied in alignment with the aims and objectives of the study.

2.1 Formulation of the research question

The articles were retrieved from various databases, including Scopus, Web of Science, Lens, and Google Scholar; subsequently, the PRISMA framework was used to identify the specific articles. In this study, the search process involved three primary databases, viz., Scopus, Web of Science and Lens. Alongside these databases, Google Scholar was used as a supporting database. Primary databases offered multiple benefits, including advanced search queries, multi-disciplinary areas, and broader content coverage from journals on climate change (Gusenbauer & Haddaway, 2020; Shaffril et al., 2021). Despite concerns expressed by studies (Halevi et al., 2017) about its failure to control quality, studies asserted that Google Scholar might serve as a strong supporting database with approximately 389 million documents retrievable from its database (Gusenbauer, 2019; Haddaway et al., 2015). The study used the PICo framework to formulate the research question (Panja et al., 2024; Shaffril et al., 2020). The three concepts included in PICo were population or problem, interest, and context. For this study, the population was livestock farmers, its interest was adaptation strategies, and its context was South Asian livestock farming under changing climates. Including these three concepts raised a question: What are the adaptation strategies followed by the livestock farmers in South Asia to cope with climate change?

2.2 Search of documents and article screening

Documents in the primary databases were searched (as mentioned in earlier sections) using a search query developed. The search was made in the advanced search section of Google Scholar using combinations of keywords mentioned in the query for all South Asian countries. Both the searches were carried out on 8 November 2023. Identified keywords were selected based on past research (Kundu et al., 2020; Panja et al., 2024). The two PRISMA frameworks illustrated in Figure 2 assessed the papers ultimately selected for the study. A total of 699 articles were retrieved from primary databases. Criteria for the selection of articles in the primary databases were: (i) it should be research articles; (ii) the research article should have been published between 2000 and 2023; and (iii) the article should be in the English language. A total of 987 articles were retrieved from Google Scholar. Since Google Scholar has no provision for automatic sorting of scientific and review articles it was not possible (Mizik, 2021), so the criteria were limited to (i) time of publication between 2000 and 2023; and (ii) it should be in the English language. Research articles were, therefore, selected manually after retrieving articles from Google Scholar. The selection of articles was set in the timeline from 2000 to 2023 to ensure that the articles were recent but long enough to draw reasonable interpretations. After title-abstract and full-text screening, as recommended by the PRISMA protocol (Panja et al., 2024), 18 and 23 articles were selected from primary databases and Google Scholar, respectively, for final inclusion in the study, thereby bringing up a total of 41 pieces for this study. Exclusion criteria for the selection of an article were based on the ‘Population, Interest & Context’ (PICo) framework, i.e., (1) the study area not being South Asia or any countries within South Asia; (2) the study not focusing on adaptation strategies; and (3) the population of the study not being livestock farmers.

Figure 2. PRISMA framework followed for the study.

3. Results and discussion

3.1 General overview of publication dynamics of included literature

Publication trends mentioning strategies for adaptation adopted by livestock farmers in South Asia were found to be simplistic in the early 21st century. Figure 3 illustrates a growing tendency to publish more articles as the 21st century moved on, after the initial paucity. Two inflection points in the publication trend were identified: one post-2010 and the other post-2018. After both these points, the trend was observed to advance significantly. In Phase 1, only two publications were available that identified the adaptation methods employed by livestock farmers in South Asia, whereas Phase 2 saw an increase to 13 studies. But Phase 3 was remarkable in the release of 26 papers, with 2020 recording the highest output at seven articles. In Phase 3, the number of publications rose by 44.44% as compared to Phase 2, indicating heightened interest in research regarding the impacts of climate change on the livestock sector, and the necessity to identify adaptation measures against climate change.

Figure 3. Annual publication trends of the included articles.

Source: Author’s compilation.

In the 21st century, five South Asian nations – India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka – demonstrated substantial scientific contributions to the subject of this study. India has the highest number of publications (28 papers), representing approximately 68% of the total publications for the study ( Figure 4).

Figure 4. Country-wise categorisation of retrieved literature.

Source: Author’s compilation.

3.2 Keywords network analysis

Figure 5 shows keyword network analysis co-occurrence from study-related literature. The keyword network visualisation showed the complex interactions between climate stressors, climate adaptation strategies, and farming practices among South Asian smallholder livestock rearers. This multi-dimensional environmental, economic, and institutional adaptation shaped climate change resilience. Table 1 indicates the occurrences of the network’s top 20 terms and their link strength.

Figure 5. Co-occurrence network of author’s keywords for included literature.

Table 1. Top 20 keywords with their occurrences and total link strength.

The most dominant keyword in the network is ‘Adaptation’, appearing 26 times (32.91% of total keywords), as it is the core concept of the topic. Its strong association with other keywords (total link strength: 96) reflects the interconnectedness of adaptation strategies with various aspects such as climate resilience, livestock management, and farmers’ perceptions. The prominence of the term ‘Climate change’ (20 occurrences, link strength: 78) as the second most frequent keyword supports the argument that farmers’ adaptation strategies are primarily driven by climate variability and extreme events. This also indicates that research in this domain is largely problem-driven. The central presence of these two keywords serves as bridging nodes within the network, linking farmers’ perceptions, adaptation strategies, and policy considerations.

The emergence of keywords such as ‘Livestock’ (12 occurrences, link strength: 45) and ‘Dairy farmer’ (7 occurrences, link strength: 23) highlights the sensitivity of livestock, particularly dairy, to climate change, requiring specialised adaptation measures. These terms’ stronger links reflect strategies for adaptation focused on health management, feeding management, and indigenous technological knowledge. Despite its relevance, ‘Livestock disease management’ is rare, suggesting a need for research in disease management adaptation options. The keyword ‘Farmer’s perception’ (7 occurrences, link strength: 29) is well-connected, indicating that farmers’ mind sets play a major role in increasing livestock resilience to climate change. It emphasised farmer-based adaptation frameworks that account for behavioural responses, indigenous knowledge, and existing socio-economic conditions that influence decision making and enhance climate resilience. Keywords such as ‘Flood’ (4 occurrences, link strength: 14) and ‘Drought’ (2 occurrences, link strength: 7) indicated the importance of livestock sector adaptation strategies to South Asian climatic calamities. However, the network also found fewer region-specific studies on coping with climatic disasters, such as floods in Odisha and droughts in Bihar. In contrast, most studies focused on climate change’s broad impact. This showed a lack of livestock sector-specific disaster adaptation studies. Despite severe climate stressors such as flood, drought, and heat waves in this region, research generalised adaption strategies without categorising them. ‘Indigenous climate resilience practices’ revealed that traditional knowledge builds resilience, especially in fodder seed storage and animal disease management.

Keywords such as ‘India’, ‘Pakistan’, and ‘Bangladesh’ indicated country-wise geographical references and regionally varying climate adaptation in South Asia. India had more research on this study’s subject than other South Asian countries, particularly in Odisha, Bihar, Indian Sundarbans, and Punjab. This requires further study in different South Asian countries, especially in coastal and flood-prone areas where livestock suffers higher climate extremities. Policy keywords were under-represented, indicating a need for institutional support, financial aid, policy integration, and advocacy.

Figure 6 presents an overlay visualisation of the keyword network, offering a chronological perspective on the evolution of research on adaptation measures employed by smallholder livestock rearers in South Asia to minimise the impacts of climate change. Early research (2014-2018) primarily focused on fundamental topics such as ‘climatic variability’, ‘livestock’, and ‘farmer perception’, whereas studies from 2018 onwards incorporated terms such as ‘risk management strategies’ and ‘agro-climatic zones’, reflecting a deeper investigation into localised climatic adaptation strategies. Recent studies have revealed a growing emphasis on technology-driven and policy-oriented adaptation solutions, highlighted by the rise of terms such as ‘climate-smart agriculture,’ ‘institutional innovation,’ ‘climate change policy,’ and ‘determinants’. The new inclusion of ‘Nepal’ signifies the acceptance of another geographically diverse area apart from India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh within the South Asian region. Including keywords such as ‘flood’ and ‘rainfall anomaly’ signifies research focused on adaptation techniques for specific climatic catastrophes in contrast to generalised climate change adaptation tactics.

Figure 6. Overlay visualisation of keyword network analysis.

3.3 Classification of adaptation strategies based on themes

The adaptation strategies identified in the reviewed studies were categorised into eight themes: thermal stress management, feeding management, fodder management, housing management, health management, breeding management, organisational management, and transformative strategies ( Table 2). These domains encompassed 62 adaptation techniques that were effectively identified by the study from the demarcated literature.

Table 2. Developed themes and distribution of included literature based on themes.

Among eight themes, thermal stress management strategies were reported most frequently (65.85%) by the research, followed by fodder management strategies, breeding management strategies, and transformative strategies, each cited by 46.34% of the studies. Thermal stress – encompassing both heat stress and cold stress – represents the most severe climatic effects on livestock. Health management strategies were documented in 43.90% of the research, while feeding management techniques were noted in 39.02% of the studies. Both housing management methods and organisational management strategies were reported by 36.59% of the studies, each.

3.3.1 Thermal stress management strategies

Given the escalating effects of climate change, the livestock sector must implement appropriate thermal stress management strategies. Temperature extremes in a wide range of areas in South Asia pose significant threats to animal health, productivity, and overall well-being. Implementing climate-resilient thermal stress management strategies demonstrate commitment to a healthy and robust livestock sector. Research has identified 14 strategies for managing thermal stress among livestock farmers in South Asia.

Livestock rearers set up shade arrangements for animals during hot days as an immediate adaptation strategy to protect animals from heat stress (Gautam et al., 2011; Mathur et al., 2019; Poudel, 2015; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; Shahbaz et al., 2020; York et al., 2017). Smallholder farmers in South Asia who predominantly rear animals depend on grazing for sustenance, heightening their vulnerability to the effects of heat waves. Shade prevents animals from sweating excessively during the day (Goswami et al., 2020). In addition, animals preferred shade when the THI exceeded 88 (Mathur et al., 2019). Shade could either be constructed or natural with tree shade (Goswami et al., 2020; York et al., 2017). Studies indicated that farmers, especially in South Asia, preferred tree shade due to its cost-saving aspect and accessibility (Gautam et al., 2011; Mathur et al., 2019; Poudel, 2015; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; Shahbaz et al., 2020). Evapotranspiration and airflow have a cooling effect under naturally growing tree shade, especially under trees such as Khejri (Prosopis cineraria) as compared to manufactured shade (Mathur et al., 2019). Social forestry was also adopted as a community approach for preserving the microclimate and sheltering animals during grazing (Maiti et al., 2014).

Along with providing shade, measures such as avoiding long-distance grazing (Goswami et al. 2020), and preferring stall feeding (Poudel, 2015) during heat stress became some of the significant heat stress adaptation strategies. During heat stress, animals face higher body temperature and reduced feed intake, hence feeding pattern and schedule adjustments are important adaptation mechanisms (Kant et al., 2017; Maiti et al., 2014; Mathur et al., 2019; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; R. K. Singh et al., 2013b). Change in feeding patterns include variation in proportion of roughage and concentrate in the feed of the animal to supplement the energy loss of the animal due to heat stress (Kant et al., 2017). Studies indicated that livestock rearers came to believe that daytime feeding in high summer causes restlessness, discomfort, and uneasiness; hence, changing feeding schedules was a key adaptation strategy (Maiti et al., 2014; Mathur et al., 2019; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016). Animals could be fed during cooler times like late afternoon, early morning, and night (Goswami et al., 2020; Maiti et al., 2014). Feeding animals in the morning or late morning was not recommended due to increased heat generation during the day and high outside temperatures (Upadhyay et al., 2009). Changing the grazing time of the animals (Maiti et al., 2014; Mathur et al., 2019) and altering the frequency and duration of grazing (Suresh et al., 2010) were also found to be adopted by the farmers to protect the animals from heat stress. Similarly, milking animals during cooler hours was a key adaptation strategy (Goswami et al., 2020).

Livestock farmers came to realize that rising body temperatures of animals affected due to heat stress could be treated by extra bathing/showering the animals (Maiti et al., 2014; Poudel, 2015; Shahbaz et al., 2020; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b; York et al., 2017); and by setting up mechanical cooling facilities to change the microclimate inside the shed, including the use of fans, coolers and sprinklers (Chand et al., 2018; Goswami et al., 2020; Maiti et al., 2014; Paramesh et al., 2022; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; Shahbaz et al., 2020; York et al., 2017). Reducing body temperature is critical for livestock, particularly for buffalos, as silent heat stress lowers productivity and reproduction. It is helpful to have animal housing with mud roofs and floors during heat stress (Shahbaz et al., 2020). Animal dehydration caused by heat stress was effectively managed through the consistent provision of clean and fresh drinking water (F. Ahmed et al., 2012; Dutta et al., 2020; Gautam et al., 2011; Goswami et al., 2018; Maiti et al., 2014; Mathur et al., 2019; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; Shahbaz et al., 2020; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Farmers stored and made use of rainwater harvested during the rainy season to reduce water scarcity in the scorching summer (Dhakal et al., 2016; Goswami et al., 2020; Rayamajhi & Manandhar, 2020; S. Singh et al., 2012).

Thermal stress induced by cold waves is prevalent in numerous areas of South Asia. Jute and cotton coverings were placed on the animals (Fahim & Sikder, 2022; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b), and bedding in the shed was composed of straw, corn husk, and field waste during dry winter conditions, while materials such as husk brick, sand, and rice straw were utilised during extreme wet and rainy conditions to insulate the animals’ body temperature and shield them from the adverse effects of cold waves (Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Shahbaz et al., 2020; S. Singh et al., 2012). Animal bodies were also massaged with mustard oil in rural South Asia to keep cattle healthy amid the harsh cold (Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b).

South Asian livestock farmers in adverse climatic regions also moved their animals to safer and more pleasant climates during harsh seasons (Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Rayamajhi & Manandhar, 2020; R. K. Singh et al., 2013b). It included shifting the animals to higher ground during floods (Anitha et al., 2023; Behera et al., 2021), or moving them to a friend’s/relative’s house or community shelter where safe shelter and fodder are available (Goswami et al., 2020; Silambarasan et al., 2022).

3.3.2 Feeding management strategies

Effective livestock feeding management strategies are vital for combatting climate change. It impacts forage, nutrition, and water resources, thereby threatening animal health and productivity. Given this uncertainty, flexible feeding practices are essential. The study identified five feeding management strategies implemented by livestock producers in South Asia.

Temperature, precipitation, and heat stress caused by climate change reduce feed consumption and animal nutrition, and thus productivity. Supplemental concentrate is a prevalent feeding strategy (Dutta et al., 2020; Goswami et al., 2018; Maiti et al., 2014; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Ranasinghe et al., 2023a; Shahbaz et al., 2020; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Increased concentrate in feed offsets energy requirements for heat dissipation. It provides concentrated protein and micronutrients for sustaining energy balance and essential physiological functions. A deficiency of essential minerals and vitamins frequently arises from the overall consequences of climate change and its detrimental influence on animals. Animals lose sodium and potassium due to excessive perspiration (Goswami et al., 2018). Consequently, farmers enrich animal feed with vitamins, minerals, and chemicals (Das et al., 2020, 2022; Dutta et al., 2020; Goswami et al., 2018; Kant et al., 2017; Maiti et al., 2014; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Verma et al., 2020; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b; Yadav & Ghosh, 2023). Other common materials used are common salt and molasses (Dutta et al., 2020; Goswami et al., 2018). Farmers feed livestock a compound of block/urea molasses mineral, block/total mixed ration to offer a balanced diet (Goswami et al., 2018; Kant et al., 2017; P. R. Singh et al., 2004).

Disasters such as drought, floods, and cyclones affect animal feed and fodder. Smallholder farmers also prioritise food crops over fodder, causing shortages (K. M. Singh et al., 2013a). Consequently, farmers implemented the practice of feeding crop residue to livestock as a significant adaptation strategy in response to climate change (Anitha et al., 2023; Goswami et al., 2018; Maiti et al., 2014; Poudel, 2015; P. R. Singh et al., 2004; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Animal feed included crop residues of cereals and millets (Poudel, 2015), hay (Maiti et al., 2014), immature paddy crops, maize and tree leaves (Goswami et al., 2018). Indigenous feeding practices involved administering oil, salt, and jaggery to animals as supplements to reduce the effects of climate change, especially heat stress. Oil helps animals generate bypass protein and is a fat supplement, while jaggery is a popular glucose supplement under heat stress.

3.3.3 Fodder management strategies

Fodder management practices are crucial for cattle in the context of climate change. Fodder quality, availability, and distribution depend on weather, which in turn affect animal nutrition and welfare. Fodder management must include efficient forage cultivation, preservation, and supplementation to ensure a nutritious feed source. The study identified nine fodder management practices used by South Asian livestock farmers.

Preserving fodder to address the scarcity arising from climate change was the most significant long-term management strategy (Fahim & Sikder, 2022; Goswami et al., 2018; Hossain & Paul, 2019; Kant et al., 2017; Poudel, 2015; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; Rayamajhi & Manandhar, 2020; Roy et al., 2014; Silambarasan et al., 2022; S. Singh et al., 2012). One of the most often utilised techniques for preserving fodder was the production of hay and silage (Goswami et al., 2018; Kant et al., 2017; Roy et al., 2014; Silambarasan et al., 2022; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Hay is produced through the process of sun-drying predominantly fodder crops, whereas silage is produced through anaerobic fermentation (Lancaster et al., 1977). Livestock farmers protect fodder crops from floods, cyclones, and other weather by making hay and silage in off-seasons. Additionally, it supplies feed crops to climate-vulnerable South Asian regions where it can’t be grown. Growing green fodder in the off-season (Paramesh et al., 2022) and planting multi-purpose fodder trees and grasses such as tanki (Bauhinia purpurea L.), kutmiro (Litsea polyantha Juss.), and khanayo (Ficus cunia) near livestock sheds provide a continuous supply of fodder (Das et al., 2022; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Poudel, 2015; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; S. Singh et al., 2012). The study showed that South Asian smallholder livestock rearers without land or resources used community lands for fodder production, especially during scarcity (Das et al., 2020; Yadav & Ghosh, 2023).

Apart from preservation, fodder cultivation practices were also important. Fodder producers in South Asia adopted many strategies including changes in the cropping system, pattern, and crop calendar (Abbas et al., 2019; F. Ahmed et al., 2012; Goswami et al., 2020; S. Singh et al., 2012). Transitioning from monoculture to mixed farming is sufficiently sustainable to fulfil food and fodder requirements, diversify income, and respond to climatic challenges (Ahmed et al., 2012). A primary crop intercropped with a legume species can provide financial benefits and conserve feed for livestock in the same field during adverse weather. Switching from monoculture to intercropping improves soil fertility, reduces pesticide use, and promotes livestock health.

Growing hazard-resistant fodder varieties was crucial for ensuring South Asia’s fodder supplies (Goswami et al., 2020; Silambarasan et al., 2022; S. Singh et al., 2012). Studies have identified cultivation of flood-tolerant fodder crop varieties such as CoFs29 (sorghum variety) and para grass (Silambarasan et al., 2022), drought-tolerant fodder crops such as sorghum (jowar), cluster bean (guar), pearl millet (bajra), maize (makka), etc., (Goswami et al., 2020). Cultivation of early maturing fodder varieties such as local varieties of maize (‘Sathi’, ‘Jaunpur’) that can mature in 70 to 80 days, sorghum hybrid varieties such as ‘Pusa Chari Hybrid 109’ had an impact on fodder security among the local farmers in the context of climate change. During droughts, perennial fodder crops such as Subabul (Leucaena leucocephala) were grown (Goswami et al., 2020). Incorporating fodder as a substantial element within the Integrated Farming System also facilitated the farmers’ establishment of viable livestock farms.

Even while farmers were trying to build a sustainable fodder production system to counteract climate change, they had to buy fodder from the market and other farmers when in need, especially after floods, droughts, tropical cyclones, etc., (Fahim & Sikder, 2022; Goswami et al., 2018; Kant et al., 2017; Poudel, 2015; Ranasinghe et al., 2023).

3.3.4 Health management strategies

In addition to directly affecting livestock conditions, climate change also leads to proliferation of diseases among cattle. It promotes sickness, threatening animal welfare. Better healthcare, disease surveillance, and sanitation are necessary to mitigate climate-induced health risks. The study found seven adaptation strategies for livestock health management used by South Asian livestock rearers.

Heat waves, droughts, and floods most likely promote infectious diseases and parasitism in livestock (Abbas et al., 2019) and cause diseases such as black quarter, foot and mouth disease (FMD), haemorrhagic septicaemia, goat pox, etc., (Das et al., 2020; Paramesh et al., 2022; Yadav & Ghosh, 2023). It was found that prophylactic use of medications and preventative vaccinations against animal diseases was one of the most significant adaptation techniques implemented by farmers (Abbas et al., 2019; Anitha et al., 2023; Das et al., 2020, 2022; Hossain & Paul, 2019; Paramesh et al., 2022; Shahbaz et al., 2020; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b; Yadav & Ghosh, 2023). Indian vaccination campaigns to eliminate FMD and Brucella have greatly expanded cattle immunisation (Paramesh et al., 2022). Vulnerable climate and unhygienic feed and water breed worms. Deworming livestock is a crucial health management approach (Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Maintaining animal cleanliness, including shed cleaning and disinfectant application, is a crucial adaptation strategy for farmers (Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Using mosquito nets to prevent vector-borne diseases is another effective adaptation approach (Maiti et al., 2014).

For centuries farmers had consistently employed traditional knowledge as a means to combat cattle diseases (Anitha et al., 2023; Dutta et al., 2020; Goswami et al., 2020; Rana et al., 2019; Usman et al., 2023). Wallowing in water bodies by buffalos was considered by livestock rearers as the most effective way of treating heat stress (Goswami et al., 2020). Indigenous knowledge for livestock ailments includes giving neem (Azadirachta indica) and babul (Acacia arabica) leaf paste for worms, jackfruit and bamboo leaves for diarrhoea, drek plant leaves for skin diseases, tobacco leaf extract for scabies in sheep and goats, and many more (Dutta et al., 2020; Rana et al., 2019). Maintaining hygiene and producing clean milk helps prevent severe animal infections such as mastitis and salmonellosis (Chand et al., 2018). Regular livestock health management consulting with a licensed veterinarian was identified to be an essential approach (Chand et al., 2018; Dhakal et al., 2016; Fahim & Sikder, 2022; Goswami et al., 2018).

3.3.5 Breeding management strategies

Breeding management tactics are key to long-term climate change adaptation for livestock animals. Livestock sustainability in changing climate depends on breed improvement. It strengthens resilience to environmental uncertainty. The research has identified six strategies that fall under breeding management strategies.

In regions of South Asia where climate change vulnerability was higher, farmers were found to rear and promote indigenous breeds as an adaptation strategy (F. Ahmed et al., 2012; Anitha et al., 2023; Darjee et al., 2023; Gautam et al., 2011; Goswami et al., 2020; Maiti et al., 2014; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Silambarasan et al., 2022; S. Singh et al., 2012; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). While indigenous varieties possess the capacity to adjust with the adverse conditions of climate change and fodder unavailability (F. Ahmed et al., 2012), crossbred animals such as Holstein, Frisian, Jersey, etc., lack this ability (Maiti et al., 2014). Indigenous animals, however, are frequently low in productivity. Additionally, high-yielding livestock breeds were crucial to meet the livestock rearers’ financial requirements (Das et al., 2022). Thus, raising crossbred animals or enhancing local nondescript breeds with crossbred animals or proven indigenous breeds like Red Sindhi, Sahiwal, Gir, etc., with higher output potential was necessary (Chand et al., 2013; Gautam et al., 2011; Maiti et al., 2014; Paramesh et al., 2022).

Research has documented artificial insemination as a significant risk-averse strategy employed by farmers in anticipation of the consequences of climate change (Chand et al., 2018; Verma et al., 2020). Livestock breeders employed good semen for greater calf production (Chand et al., 2018). Healthy male animals were sought for natural mating in hilly and distant places where artificial insemination was impossible (Chand et al., 2018). Livestock breeders had been traditionally exchanging bulls between regions to prevent inbreeding depression.

3.3.6 Housing management strategies

Effective housing management measures are required to safeguard the cattle industry in South Asia from the adverse effects of climate hazards and global warming. Animal shelters minimise climate change impacts by providing optimal living conditions. Studies have shown that South Asian livestock farmers adopted several housing management strategies. The most crucial was building permanent sheds or pucca shelters to protect livestock from floods, excessive rainfall, heat waves, cyclones, etc., (Anitha et al., 2023; Behera et al., 2021; Fahim & Sikder, 2022; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a). It was discovered that farmers residing in flood-prone or coastal regions constructed elevated animal barns to shield these dwellings from the effects of flooding (Anitha et al., 2023; Behera et al., 2021; Hossain & Paul, 2019; Silambarasan et al., 2022). Farmers considered cross-ventilation of the shed as a critical prerequisite for constructing climate-resistant housing (Dutta et al., 2020; Ranasinghe et al., 2023). It promotes airflow, removes odours, moisture, dust, gases, and bacteria. Additionally, it improves air quality and cooling, which helps preserve the shed’s THI value (Dutta et al., 2020). Increasing the roof height of the shed also plays a role.

Altering the shed’s microclimate was a crucial adaptation approach for climate-proofing the shelter (Goswami et al., 2020; Maiti et al., 2014; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016). Mechanical cooling devices, including fans, coolers, and sprinklers, were utilised by farmers to safeguard their livestock against heat stress. They were also using locally obtainable materials such as coconut leaves and straw on the roof to lower the shed’s temperature (Maiti et al., 2014). The microclimate of the animal shed could also be modified by planting trees in its vicinity (Parameswaranaik et al., 2016; Poudel, 2015; Shahbaz et al., 2020; S. Singh et al., 2012). A reduction in the internal temperature was also observed when water was sprayed onto the shed during periods of high temperature (Hossain & Paul, 2019). Fumigating the animal shed to avoid vector control diseases, parasites, and air quality was another approach beneficial for animal protection (Goswami et al., 2020; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Fumigation was done either by smoking with cow dung and wood (Goswami et al. 2020) or by using Ajwain and neem (Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a).

3.3.7 Organizational management strategies

Effective organisational management methods are essential for cattle industry sustainability in the face of climate change. Organisations need to implement a comprehensive management framework that encompasses resource allocation, risk mitigation, and contingency planning to adapt to climatic uncertainties. Effective organisational support promotes cattle climate resilience. Even though nine adaption techniques have been established in organisational management strategies for livestock rearers in South Asia, they are interconnected and linked.

Various organisations significantly contributed to the improvement of livestock rearers’ knowledge and adaptability to climate-resilient services through the provision of training (F. Ahmed et al., 2012; Verma et al., 2020). Consultation services are needed for feed formulation, fodder production and conservation, feed supplementation, housing management, animal record keeping, waste management, clean milk production, health management, etc., (Verma et al., 2020). Training and demonstrations help farmers learn any required skill and modify their viewpoint on climate-resilient practices. It is the best method for successfully implementing climate-resilient practices. Improvement of livestock and veterinary extension services in this context become highly significant and crucial (Dhakal et al., 2016; P. R. Singh et al., 2004; Verma et al., 2020). Livestock extension services that are driven by demand can effectively cater to the interests and requirements of livestock rearers (Verma et al., 2020).

Another important aspect of organizational management is provision of financial support for livestock rearers, especially smallholder farmers. Whether in emergencies and losses or normal conditions, financial support help farmers combat climate change. In order to protect the interests of farmers, policy-making institutions had, from time to time, implemented a variety of risk aversion measures, including weather-based livestock insurance schemes, credit facilities, and subsidies (Chand et al., 2018; Gautam et al., 2011; Goswami et al., 2020; Maiti et al., 2014; Silambarasan et al., 2022). Adversity caused by climate hazards may result in significant financial repercussions for animal rearers. The provision of livestock insurance provides a degree of financial security in the event of accidental deaths of animals due to climate hazards (Maiti et al., 2014). The formation of self-help groups addresses gender equity among livestock rearers. Also, it was found to support the farmers in developing animal products and selling them in the market (R. K. Singh et al., 2013b).

Organisational support was also found to have a role in enhancing the absorptive capacity of farmers in building resilience. Arranging community vaccination camps after a disaster (Roy et al., 2014), shifting animals to a safe place such as a cyclone shelter, or government building or some elevated place (Anitha et al., 2023; Behera et al., 2021; Goswami et al., 2020; Rasool et al., 2020; Silambarasan et al., 2022), arranging for relief help from the government, NGOs, etc., and ensuring proper reach to those in need (Gautam et al., 2011; Rasool et al., 2020; Silambarasan et al., 2022; R. K. Singh et al., 2013b), were some of the essential roles that come under organisational management strategies.

3.3.8 Transformative strategies

The term ‘transformative strategies’ often denotes approaches that signify transitioning from the existing system to a new one. Studies have identified five strategies under transformative strategies. Reduction of herd size was found to be a significant strategy (Abbas et al., 2019; Anitha et al., 2023; Goswami et al., 2020; Joshi et al., 2023; Maiti et al., 2014; Poudel, 2015; Ranasinghe et al., 2023; Rasool et al., 2020; Shahbaz et al., 2020; S. Singh et al., 2012; Suresh et al., 2010; Yadav, Maiti, Jha, et al., 2022a; Yadav, Maiti, Meena, et al., 2022b). Smallholder livestock rearers, especially those in climatically fragile South Asian regions such as coastal India or flood-prone Bihar, have to limit their herd size due to feed and fodder shortages, financial crises, etc. Selling animals during a crisis was another way households raised money for emergency costs. In the same context, another prevalent transformative strategy involved the shift from large ruminants such as cattle and buffalo to small ruminants like goats and sheep (Dutta et al., 2020; Goswami et al., 2020; Kattumuri et al., 2017; Maiti et al., 2014; Parameswaranaik et al., 2016). Farmers benefited from this in two ways: first, small ruminants served as an economic asset, protecting them from catastrophic financial loss and fodder instability during a crisis. Second, these small animals are comparatively inexpensive to raise and sell.

Searching for alternate jobs or other off-farm income activities (Abbas et al., 2019; Chand et al., 2018; Gautam et al., 2011; Goswami et al., 2020; Maiti et al., 2014; S. Singh et al., 2012) and social migration (Abbas et al., 2019; Goswami et al., 2020; Kant et al., 2017; Maiti et al., 2014; S. Singh et al., 2012) to sustain the household was also identified by the studies as a growing adaptation strategy by the rearers in the face of intensifying climate change.

4. Conclusion, implications and policy recommendations

Climate change poses a significant threat to livestock-based livelihoods in South Asia, impacting the productive and reproductive performance of livestock, which in turn affects food security. Adaptation strategies for the livestock sector are, thus, essential for building sustainability and meeting food security. This study systematically analysed existing literature and categorised adaptation strategies mentioned therein into eight thematic domains, viz., thermal stress management, feeding management, fodder management, health management, breeding management, housing management, organisational management, and transformative strategies. It was observed that thermal stress management and fodder management strategies were the most implemented adaptation measures. This highlights the serious threat from heat and cold stress prevailing in the region and impacting livestock, along with the existing feed insecurity due to climatic variabilities and extremities.

While several adaptation strategies were identified in this study and reviewed scrupulously based on their type, their use and implementation, their adoption and effectiveness vary depending on geographical contexts, socio-economic conditions, climatic exposures and prevailing hazards. Most livestock rearers in the smallholder category lack financial resources, institutional support, and accessibility to better climate-resilient strategies, which constrain their adaptive capacity. Furthermore, traditional or indigenous knowledge, which plays a crucial role in shaping the adaptation decisions of the rearers, lacks integration into formal adaptation policies and frameworks. It was observed that the majority of these studies came from India and had a very low proportion from other South Asian countries, indicating a lack of comprehensive policy and research frameworks for livestock climate adaptation in those regions.

Given South Asia’s diverse agro-climatic conditions, formulating adaptation strategies have to be context-specific rather than one-size-fits-all solutions. This further necessitates multi-level policy interventions that could bridge the gap between scientific research, policy frameworks and implementation of adaptation strategies at the ground level. It also calls for a proper collaborative approach between the government, research bodies and local-level development agencies to facilitate the scaling up of cost-effective climate-smart livestock adaptation strategies.

Policy recommendations for improvement of the livestock sector of South Asia with regard to climate variability and extreme events include the following:

First, climate-smart adaptation strategies for livestock should be mainstreamed. This includes the development of region-specific adaptation pathways that consider the local needs and interests of the farmers and facilitate knowledge co-productions. These regional adaptation plans should be integrated into the national-level policy framework. As regards the regional adaptation plan, it should also include developing and promoting heat-tolerant livestock breeds, drought-tolerant fodder varieties, and standard feeding patterns.

Second, financial and institutional support for the rearers should be enhanced. This includes strengthening climate-based livestock insurance for livestock farmers after assessing region-wise climatic risk and vulnerability. This can ensure protection of farmers and facilitate risk minimisation against climate-induced losses. Financial support, including credit facilities, is to be promoted for adopting strategies that require high investment, such as building climate-proof shelters or rainwater harvesting facilities.

Third, leveraging technology and research to improve the adaptive capacity of farmers. This includes developing local-level climate-smart innovations and infrastructures such as early warning systems, artificial insemination centres and better healthcare facilities.

Fourth, capacity building must be strengthened, and knowledge and technology transfer must be made efficient. Policies should be advocated to develop community-based extension programmes and agencies that provide extension advisory services. This should also include a framework to integrate all genders and strengthen local youth.

Fifth, disaster preparedness must be planned well in advance. Besides providing immediate aid and grants, the approach should also include speedy treatment of livestock after a disaster. A policy should also be advocated to prepare infrastructure (such as cyclone shelters) and train the local-level disaster teams to minimise the impacts of disasters. Livestock adaptation strategies should be integrated into national disaster risk reduction policies so as to ensure proactive responses rather than reactive responses across all South Asian countries.

Data availability

We have no data to declare as there is no primary data associated with this article.
